1: wayyēṣĕʾû kol-bĕnê yiśrāʾēl wayyiqqāhēl hāʿēdāh kĕʾîš ʾeḥād — 'Then all the sons of Israel came out and the congregation assembled as one man.' kĕʾîš ʾeḥād (H259+H376, as one man) — the covenant-unity idiom that normally signals holy-war consensus (cf. 1 Samuel 11:7; Ezra 3:1). Bitterly ironic: the nation achieves its only unanimous assembly not against the Canaanites (see Judges 1:1-2, where the campaign begins without full tribal unity) but in civil war. dan ʿad bĕʾēr-šebaʿ (from Dan to Beersheba) — the traditional north-to-south formula for all Israel appears for the first time in Judges here, again in service of internal conflict rather than covenant conquest.
2: pinnôt kol-hāʿām kol šibṭê yiśrāʾēl biqhal ʿam hāʾĕlōhîm — 'the chiefs of all the people, all the tribes of Israel, in the assembly of the people of God.' ʿam hāʾĕlōhîm (people of God) — the generic divine name Elohim rather than the covenant name YHWH; the phrase occurs in very few places in the narrative literature. The use of Elohim here may signal distance: Israel is still the people of God, but the divine intimacy of the covenant name is absent as they move toward fratricide. ʾarbāʿāh mēʾôt ʾelep règlî šōlēp ḥāreb (four hundred thousand swordsmen on foot) — the census language evokes Numbers; the army is enormous, dwarfing even David's forces.
3: wayyišmĕʿû bĕnê binyāmîn kî ʿālû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl hammiṣpāh — 'The Benjamites heard that the sons of Israel had gone up to Mizpah.' Benjamin's posture throughout Judges 20 is reactive, never initiating dialogue. The assembly summons them to give account (daḇrû-nā lĕʿênênû, tell us) — a forensic challenge. Their silence until Judges 20:13 (refusal to hand over the guilty men) is their decision to stand with their own kinsmen rather than with covenant justice, the inverse of what Judah does in the opening chapter.
4: wayaʿan hāʾîš hallēwî ʾîš hāʾiššāh hannirṣāḥāh — 'The Levite, the husband of the murdered woman, answered.' nirṣāḥāh (H7523, murdered/slain — the Piel of rṣḥ, which appears in the Decalogue prohibition: lōʾ tirṣāḥ, you shall not murder, Exodus 20:13). The Levite characterizes his concubine's death with the Decalogue's forbidden act — framing the assembly's deliberation in covenant-law terms. His retelling (Judges 20:4-7) softens his own role while maximizing the outrage; the text does not editorialize, but the reader who knows Judges 19 notices the omissions.
5: wayyāqumû ʿālay baʿălê haggiḇĕʿāh wayyāsōḇḇû ʿālay babbayit layllāh dimmû lĕhārgēnî wĕʾet-pilagtî ʿinnû — 'The men of Gibeah rose against me and surrounded the house at night. They intended to kill me, and my concubine they violated.' dimmû (H1819, thought/plotted — dāmāh) — the verb implies premeditated malice; they had a plan. ʿinnû (H6031, afflict/violate — same root as the affliction vocabulary of Egyptian slavery, Exodus 1:11-12; Deuteronomy 26:6) — the rape of the concubine is described with the same term used for Israel's oppression in Egypt, a bitter inversion: Israel's oppressors are now other Israelites.
6: wāʾōḥaz bĕpîlagtî wāʾănattĕḥehā wāʾĕšallĕḥāh bĕkol-śĕdēh naḥălat yiśrāʾēl kî ʿāśû zimmāh ûnĕbālāh bĕyiśrāʾēl — 'I took my concubine and cut her in pieces and sent her through all the territory of Israel's inheritance, for they committed outrage and folly in Israel.' zimmāh (H2154, lewdness/outrage — especially sexual crime; Leviticus 18:17, Judges 19:29; Ezekiel 16:27) and nĕbālāh (H5039, folly/disgrace — the covenantal crime word from Genesis 34:7, Shechem and Dinah; the act that violates covenant community) — the pairing of these two terms indicts Gibeah with the full weight of covenant-crime vocabulary. The same pairing appears in Jeremiah 29:23 for those who commit adultery with their neighbor's wife — it is the vocabulary of the gravest social-covenant violations.
7: hinnĕkem kullĕkem bĕnê yiśrāʾēl hābû lākem dābār wāʿēṣāh hāllōm — 'Look, all of you are the sons of Israel; give your verdict and counsel here.' dābār wāʿēṣāh (verdict and counsel) — forensic deliberative terms; ʿēṣāh (H6098) is the counsel sought from wisdom or elders. The Levite throws the deliberation entirely to the assembly, making himself plaintiff without offering a solution — which will prove critical, since the assembly's decisions in Judges 21 have no divine warrant.
8: wayyāqom kol-hāʿām kĕʾîš ʾeḥād lēʾmōr lōʾ nēlek ʾîš lĕʾōhŏlô wĕlōʾ-nāsûr ʾîš lĕḇêtô — 'All the people rose as one man, saying: not one will go to his tent, and not one will return to his house.' The repetition of kĕʾîš ʾeḥād hammers the theme. The oath language (lōʾ-nāsûr, will not return) anticipates the oath problem in Judges 21: rash collective vows sworn in assembly will constrain Israel's options and generate further atrocity. The rhetorical pattern — unanimous declaration at an assembly — is the same structure as Ezra 10:12 (community covenanting to put away foreign wives) and Nehemiah 8:6 (all the people answer Amen), but here it generates violence rather than reformation.
9: wĕʿattāh zeh haddābār ʾăšer naʿăśeh laggibĕʿāh bĕgôrāl ʿālâ ʿālêhā — 'And now this is the thing we will do to Gibeah: we will go up against it by lot.' bĕgôrāl (H1486, by lot) — the lot as a decision-making mechanism is specifically associated with distributing the inheritance (Joshua 15-21; Numbers 26:55) and with seeking YHWH's guidance (Proverbs 16:33: the lot is cast into the lap but its every decision is from YHWH). Using the lot here implies (without confirming) divine guidance — but there is no explicit oracle, no Urim and Thummim invoked.
10: wĕlāqaḥnû ʿăśārāh ʾănāšîm lĕmēʾāh lĕkol šibṭê yiśrāʾēl ûmēʾāh lĕʾelep ûʾelep lĕrĕbābāh lāleket lāqaḥat ṣēdāh lāʿām — a logistics verse for provisioning the army: 10% selected as supply-train. ṣēdāh (H6720, provision/food supply) — the same word used for the provision Jacob sends ahead to Joseph (Genesis 42:25) and for Joshua's army (Joshua 9:11). The detailed military organization signals a serious campaign — Israel is mobilizing as for a major external war.
11: wayyēʾāsep kol-ʾîš yiśrāʾēl ʾel-hāʿîr kĕʾîš ʾeḥād ḥăḇērîm — 'So all the men of Israel gathered against the city, united as one man, allies.' ḥăḇērîm (H2270, bound together/allies — the root ḥbr = to bind or join) — describes the solidarity of the coalition. The word also appears in Exodus 26:3 for the curtains of the tabernacle being joined together — a unity image. Three times in this chapter (Judges 20:1, 8, 11) Israel is called kĕʾîš ʾeḥād; the repetition is the narrator's way of marking the tragic irony.
12: wayyišlĕḥû šibṭê yiśrāʾēl ʾănāšîm bĕkol-šēḇeṭ binyāmîn lēʾmōr mah hārāʿāh hazzōʾt ʾăšer nihyĕtāh bākem — 'The tribes of Israel sent men through all the tribe of Benjamin, saying: What is this evil that has happened among you?' rāʿāh (H7451, evil) — the standard covenant-violation term. The phrasing echoes the legal rhetoric of Deuteronomy 17:12 and Judges 22:21-24 where the community is charged to purge evil (bāʿar hārāʿ) from their midst. The tribes are applying Deuteronomic law to a Levitical crime — the correct procedure, but Benjamin's refusal (Judges 20:13) puts the tribe outside the covenant community.
13: wĕʿattāh tĕnû ʾet-hāʾănāšîm bĕnê-ḇĕlîyaʿal ʾăšer bĕgibĕʿāh ûnĕmîtēm wĕnĕbāʾărāh rāʿāh miyyiśrāʾēl wĕlōʾ ʾābû bĕnê binyāmîn lišmōaʿ bĕqôl ʾăḥêhem bĕnê yiśrāʾēl — 'Hand over the men of Belial in Gibeah so we may put them to death and purge the evil from Israel. But the Benjamites were not willing to listen to the voice of their brothers.' bĕnê bĕlîyaʿal (sons of Belial — H1100, literally sons of worthlessness/destructiveness; the same term applied to the Sodomite-like men in 19:22 and to Eli's sons, 1 Samuel 2:12). bāʿar rāʿāh (purge the evil) — the identical phrase from Deuteronomy 21:21, Judges 22:21-24 for removing covenant pollutants; the formula marks capital punishment as a community-cleansing act. Benjamin's refusal to hand over the guilty is the decisive covenant break: they choose tribal solidarity over covenant justice.
14: wayyēʾāsĕpû bĕnê-binyāmîn min-hĕʿārîm giḇĕʿātāh lāṣēʾt lammilḥāmāh ʿim-bĕnê yiśrāʾēl — 'The Benjamites rallied from their cities to Gibeah to go to war against the sons of Israel.' The gathering verb ʾāsap (H622) is used throughout for covenant assembly (Numbers 11:16 for the seventy elders; Deuteronomy 33:5 for the assembly of the tribes). Here it is turned against the covenant. Gibeah becomes the Benjamin loyalists' muster point — the city of the crime becomes the fortress of defense.
15: wayyitpāqĕdû bĕnê binyāmîn bayyôm hahûʾ mēhĕʿārîm ʿĕśrîm wĕšiššāh ʾelep ʾîš šōlēp ḥāreb lĕḇad miššiḇĕʿ mēʾôt ʾîš bāḥûr — 'The Benjamites numbered twenty-six thousand swordsmen from their cities, besides seven hundred chosen men.' pāqad (H6485, muster/number) — census language, the same root used for the wilderness musters in Numbers. The precision of the numbers (26,000 + 700 = 26,700) emphasizes Benjamin's real military capability; they are outnumbered but formidable. The 700 chosen men (bāḥûr, H977 — select/young warriors) echo the elite troops in other contexts (2 Samuel 10:9; Jeremiah 49:19).
16: mikkol hāʿām hazzeh šĕbāʿ mēʾôt ʾîš bāḥûr ʾiṭṭēr yad-yĕmînô kull·ô qōlēaʿ bāʾeben ʾel-haśśaʿărāh wĕlōʾ yaḥĕṭāʾ — 'Seven hundred chosen men left-handed — every one could sling a stone at a hair's breadth and not miss.' ʾiṭṭēr yad-yĕmînô (H334, bound in his right hand = left-handed) — the same rare descriptor used for Ehud in Judges 3:15. Bitter irony: the tribe of Benjamin (ben yāmîn = son of the right hand) specializes in left-handed fighters. These 700 slingers echo the 600 armed Danites of Judges 18 and the 600 Benjamin survivors of Judges 20:47. The slinger's precision (lōʾ yaḥĕṭāʾ, will not miss — root ḥṭʾ, which is also the word for sin/miss-the-mark) creates a wordplay: they do not miss the target but they have catastrophically missed the covenant mark.
17: wĕʾanšê yiśrāʾēl hitpāqĕdû lĕḇad mibinyāmîn ʾarbāʿ mēʾôt ʾelep ʾîš šōlēp ḥāreb kull·ô ʾanšê milḥāmāh — 'The men of Israel numbered 400,000 swordsmen apart from Benjamin, all fighting men.' The coalition outnumbers Benjamin roughly Judges 15:1. The disparity sets up the theological point of Judges 20:21-25: Israel loses two battles despite overwhelming numbers before YHWH acts in the third. This mirrors the Exodus principle that divine deliverance is not by numbers (Deuteronomy 7:7; 1 Samuel 14:6) — except here the lesson is inverted: it is not a small army YHWH is vindicating but a repentant covenant community that must acknowledge its own complicity before receiving victory.
18: wayyāqumû wayyaʿălû ḇêt-ʾēl wayyišʾălû ḇĕʾĕlōhîm wayyōʾmĕrû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl mî yaaʿăle lānû ḇarĕʾšônāh lammilḥāmāh ʿim-bĕnê binyāmîn wayyōʾmer YHWH yĕhûdāh ḇarĕʾšônāh — 'They went up to Bethel and inquired of God. Judah shall go up first, YHWH said.' The inquiry verb šāʾal bĕ- (H7592, inquire of/through) is the same root used in Judges 1:1 — the book's opening inquiry — and is related to the name Saul (šāʾûl, the one asked for). The parallel with 1:1 is exact: same question (who goes first?), same answer (Judah). But here the outcome is catastrophic defeat. The narrator is using the structural echo to show that the covenant machinery is functioning (they do inquire, YHWH does answer) but the community's underlying moral condition prevents blessing. Bethel (house of God) — where Jacob encountered God (Gen 28), now a site of repeated inquiry in the crisis chapters.
19: wayyāqumû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl babbōqer wayyaḥănû ʿal-giḇĕʿāh — 'The sons of Israel rose up in the morning and encamped against Gibeah.' A transitional verse. The babbōqer (in the morning) pattern marks urgency and fresh-start energy throughout the OT (Exodus 24:4; Joshua 6:12 — Joshua rises early for Jericho). The morning action leads to the day's disaster (Judges 20:21 — 22,000 cut down).
20: wayyēṣĕʾû bĕnê binyāmîn miggibĕʿāh — 'The Benjamites came out of Gibeah.' A transitional verse; Benjamin's forces sally from their stronghold. The movement — Israel advancing, Benjamin sallying — sets up the geography of the ambush narrative that comes in Judges 20:29-44.
21: wayyaškîtû bĕnê binyāmîn mimmilḥāmāh ʾel-hayyôm hahûʾ ʾarbāʿāh wĕʿĕśrîm ʾālep îš lāʾāreṣ — 'Benjamin struck down on that day 22,000 men of Israel to the ground.' lāʾāreṣ (to the ground) — the fallen-to-ground idiom for battle death. The number is staggering: 22,000 in a single day. Theologically the defeat is unresolved — Israel went with YHWH's sanction (Judges 20:18) but suffered catastrophically. The text refuses easy answers: YHWH said go; they went; they lost. The subsequent inquiries (Judges 20:23, 27-28) show an escalating recognition that something in their own posture must change.
22: wayyitḥazzēq hāʿām ʾanšê yiśrāʾēl wayyōsipû laarōk milḥāmāh bammāqôm ʾăšer āreḵû-šām bayyôm hāriʾšôn — 'The people, the men of Israel, took courage and formed up battle again in the same place as on the first day.' wayyitḥazzēq (H2388, they strengthened themselves/took courage — Hithpael of ḥzq) — the same root used for Ezra's strengthening himself to execute reform (Ezra 7:28) and for David's encouragement at Ziklag (1 Samuel 30:6). The repetition of hammāqôm... hayyôm hāriʾšôn (the same place as on the first day) is important: they return to the exact formation, unchanged — the definition of foolishness (expecting different results).
23: wayyaʿălû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl wayyiḇkû lipnê YHWH ʿad-hāʿerev wayyišʾălû ḇĕYHWH lēʾmōr hăʾôsîp laggeshet lammilḥāmāh ʿim-bĕnê binyāmîn ʾāḥî wayyōmer YHWH ʿălû ʾēlāyw — 'They went up and wept before YHWH until evening. They asked YHWH: shall we again draw near to battle against our brother Benjamin? YHWH said: Go up against him.' Notable shift: first inquiry (Judges 20:18) used ʾĕlōhîm; now they use the covenant name YHWH. The wĕyiḇkû (they wept) marks the beginning of emotional processing; but there is no fasting yet, no burnt offering — just weeping. YHWH's answer is again affirmative, yet the second battle ends with 18,000 more dead (Judges 20:25). The two losses before victory mirror the Gideon principle: YHWH strips away false confidence before he acts.
24: wayyiqrĕbû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl ʾel-bĕnê binyāmîn bayyôm haššēnî — 'The sons of Israel advanced against the Benjamites on the second day.' Transitional verse. qāraḇ (H7126, drew near) has a cultic register (draw near to God, to the altar) as well as a military one — the dual usage here is part of the chapter's texture of inquiry and combat.
25: wayyēṣēʾ binyāmîn liḡrāʾtām miggibĕʿāh bayyôm haššēnî wayyaškîtû ḇĕḇĕnê yiśrāʾēl ʿôd šĕmônāt ʿāśār ʾelep ʾîš lāʾāreṣ kull·ô-ʾēlleh šōlĕpê ḥāreb — 'Benjamin came out of Gibeah on the second day and struck down 18,000 more men of Israel — all swordsmen.' A second crushing defeat. Total Israeli casualties after two days: 40,000. The losses equal roughly 10% of the original force, numbers proportional to a major battle. The narrator's lack of theological commentary at this point (YHWH gave the order both times) is deliberate — the tension is maintained until the third inquiry (Judges 20:26-28) resolves the theological problem by changing Israel's posture.
26: wayyaʿălû kol-bĕnê yiśrāʾēl wĕkol-hāʿām wayyāḇōʾû ḇêt-ʾēl wayyiḇkû wayyēšĕḇû šām lipnê YHWH wayyāṣûmû bayyôm hahûʾ ʿad-hāʿāreḇ wayyaʿălû ʿōlôt ûšĕlāmîm lipnê YHWH — 'They came to Bethel and wept. They sat before YHWH and fasted that day until evening and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings before YHWH.' The third inquiry is preceded by the fullest worship response yet: weeping + sitting before YHWH + fasting + ʿōlôt (burnt offerings, complete sacrifice, total dedication) + šĕlāmîm (peace/fellowship offerings, communion sacrifice). The combination of burnt and peace offerings is the signature worship of the Sinai covenant (Exodus 24:5) and of Solomon's dedication of the temple (1 Kings 8:64). Israel has moved from asking (Judges 20:18) to weeping (Judges 20:23) to full liturgical prostration (Judges 20:26). The pattern mirrors the Psalms' complaint trajectory (see Ps 22, 69): the community must exhaust its own resources before YHWH responds.
27: wayyišʾălû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl ḇĕYHWH kî-šām ʾărôn bĕrît-hāʾĕlōhîm bayyāmîm hāhēm — 'The sons of Israel inquired of YHWH — for the ark of the covenant of God was there in those days.' ʾărôn bĕrît-hāʾĕlōhîm (ark of the covenant of God) — a double-compound title combining covenant (bĕrît) and the generic divine name. The ark's presence is noted as providing the legitimacy of the inquiry: the portable sanctuary where YHWH's presence dwelt, normally associated with Shiloh (1 Samuel 1:3; Judges 3:3), is at Bethel at this point. The parenthetical note (Judges 20:27-28) grounds the narrative in an archival claim about historical location.
28: wĕpînĕḥās ben-ʾelʿāzār ben-ʾahărōn ʿōmēd lĕpānāyw bayyāmîm hāhēm wayyōʾmĕrû hăʾôsîp lāṣēʾt lammilḥāmāh ʿim-bĕnê binyāmîn ʾāḥî ʾim-ʾeḥdal wayyōmer YHWH ʿălû kî māḥār ʾettĕnnennû bĕyādekā — 'Phinehas son of Eleazar son of Aaron was ministering before it. They said: shall we go out once more or stop? YHWH said: Go up, for tomorrow I will give him into your hand.' The Phinehas identification (H6372, grandson of Aaron, Numbers 25:7 — the zealous priest who executed the Baal-Peor offenders) is a chronological anchor: if Phinehas is still alive and ministering, the Gibeah episode is placed in the early judges period, not late. This is the narrator's insertion of archival data to prevent the reader from assuming the epilogue events (Judges 17-21) are chronologically after the main narratives. The divine promise māḥār ʾettĕnnennû bĕyādekā (tomorrow I will give him into your hand) is the holy-war oracle formula used by YHWH at Jericho (Joshua 6:2: nātattî bĕyādekā) and in the Deborah narrative (Judges 4:7). Its appearance here signals that YHWH's patience with the inquiry process has been rewarded with covenant-war authorization.
29: wayyāśem yiśrāʾēl ʾōrĕḇîm ʾal-giḇĕʿāh sāḇîḇ — 'Israel set ambushers around Gibeah.' ʾōrĕḇîm (H693, ambushers — the verb ʾāraḇ, to lie in wait) — the identical ambush strategy as Joshua 8:2, 4 at Ai, where YHWH commanded the ambush against the same city type: a small fortified stronghold that lured Israel out and then was taken from the rear. The Ai-parallel is deliberate: the battle of Gibeah mirrors the capture of Ai structurally — feigned retreat, ambush, city burning, smoke signal. This positions the civil war as a reversal-application of conquest warfare: the tools YHWH gave Israel for taking Canaan are now used against a Canaanized Israelite city.
30: wayyaʿălû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl ʾel-bĕnê binyāmîn bayyôm haššĕlîšî wayyaʿarĕkû ʾel-giḇĕʿāh kĕpaʿam bĕpaʿam — 'The sons of Israel went up against the Benjamites on the third day and deployed as before.' kĕpaʿam bĕpaʿam (as time upon time/as before) — the idiom of repeated action (cf. Numbers 24:1; 1 Samuel 3:10). The identical deployment masks the ambush — the same tactic used at Ai (Joshua 8:5-6 — the ambushers hide while the main force feigns retreat).
31: wayyēṣĕʾû bĕnê binyāmîn liḡrāʾt hāʿām nittĕqû min-hāʿîr wayyāḥēllû lĕhakôt ḥălālîm kĕpaʿam bĕpaʿam — 'The Benjamites came out and were lured away from the city. They began to strike down some and kill them as before.' nittĕqû (H5423, were drawn/lured away — Niphal of nāṯaq, to tear away, draw away) — the passive form emphasizes Benjamin's lack of agency; they were drawn into the ambush, not the other way around. The ḥălālîm (H2491, the slain — those pierced/wounded) — a poetic/military term for battlefield dead.
32: wayyōʾmĕrû bĕnê binyāmîn nikkîm hēm lĕpānênû kĕbarĕʾšônāh wĕḇĕnê yiśrāʾēl ʾāmĕrû nānûsāh wĕnitteqnûhû min-hāʿîr ʾel-hammĕsillôt — 'The Benjamites said: they are falling before us as before! But Israel had said: let us flee and draw them away from the city onto the roads.' The narrator reveals the hidden strategy — Israel's words (ʾāmĕrû) are placed against Benjamin's words (wayyōʾmĕrû) in the same verse, showing the gap between what Benjamin perceives and what is actually happening. nānûsāh (let us flee) — the feigned retreat, the same tactical deception as at Ai (Joshua 8:15-16 — Joshua's army fled toward the wilderness). Deception in warfare is not condemned in the OT when used against those who have forfeited covenant standing.
33: wĕkol ʾîš yiśrāʾēl qāmû mimmĕqōmô wayyaʿarĕkû bĕḇaʿal tāmār wĕʾōrēḇ yiśrāʾēl mĕgāraʿô mimmĕqōmô mimaʿărēh-giḇaʿ — 'All the men of Israel rose from their place and formed up at Baal-tamar, and the Israelite ambush burst forth from its place at Maare-gibeah.' The coordinated maneuver: the main force wheels at Baal-tamar while the ambushers at Maare-gibeah emerge. mĕgāraʿô (from its pastureland/open ground — H4629) — a hapax in this sense; the ambushers burst out from the meadow. The precision of place names (Baal-tamar, Maare-gibeah) is unusual for Judges, suggesting the narrator is drawing on a detailed source account of the battle.
34: wayyāḇōʾû miggibĕʿāh ʿăśeret ʾălāpîm ʾîš bāḥûr mikkol yiśrāʾēl ûmilḥāmāh kāḇēdāh wĕhēm lōʾ yādĕʿû kî-nōgeaʿ ʿălêhem hārāʿāh — 'Ten thousand chosen men from all Israel struck at Gibeah itself, and the battle was heavy. But they did not know that disaster was coming upon them.' lōʾ yādĕʿû (they did not know) — the Judges phrase of fatal ignorance (Judges 3:2 — Israel did not know war; Judges 16:20 — Samson did not know YHWH had departed). Benjamin's commanders are now in the same structural position as Samson: unaware that the power that sustained them has withdrawn. nōgeaʿ (H5060, touching/striking — coming upon them) — the same root used for the plague touching Israel (Exodus 12:13).
35: wayyiggōp YHWH ʾet-binyāmîn lipnê yiśrāʾēl wayyaškîtû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl bĕbinyāmîn bayyôm hahûʾ ʿĕśrîm wĕḥāmiššāh ʾelep ûmēʾāh ʾîš kull·ô-ʾēlleh šōlĕpê ḥāreb — 'YHWH struck down Benjamin before Israel. The sons of Israel destroyed 25,100 Benjamite swordsmen that day.' wayyiggōp YHWH (H5062, YHWH struck/smote — Qal of nāgap, to strike, especially of divine plague-blow) — the first explicit statement of YHWH's active agency in this chapter. He is named in the inquiry responses but his smiting is not announced until Judges 20:35, after the full liturgical preparation of Judges 20:26. The verb nāgap is used of YHWH striking Egypt (Exodus 12:23, 27), striking the ark-bearers (1 Samuel 4:3), and striking Uzzah (2 Samuel 6:7) — it is the divine strike-verb for judgment executed directly by YHWH.
36: wayyirʾû bĕnê binyāmîn kî niggāpû wĕyittĕnû ʾanšê yiśrāʾēl mākôm lĕbinyāmîn kî bāṭĕḥû ʾel-hāʾōrēḇ ʾăšer śāmû ʾel-giḇĕʿāh — 'The Benjamites saw they were beaten, for the men of Israel gave ground relying on the ambush set at Gibeah.' A retrospective explanation inserted into the battle narrative — the narrator provides the ambush's mechanism. bāṭĕḥû (H982, they trusted/relied on — bāṭaḥ, the standard trust-confidence word of the Psalms, e.g., Psalms 22:4-5) — their trust was in the ambush, a military strategy. The placement of this trust-word in a military context echoes Psalms 20:7 (some trust in chariots) and 1 Samuel 17:45 (Goliath trusts in sword and spear).
37: wayyĕmahēr hāʾōrēḇ wayyipšĕṭû ʾel-giḇĕʿāh wayyēlek hāʾōrēḇ wayyak ʾet kol-hāʿîr leḥepê ḥāreb — 'The ambushers rushed and spread out through Gibeah; the ambush struck the whole city with the edge of the sword.' wayyĕmahēr (H4116, rushed/made haste — māhar) — the speed of the ambush's response to the signal. leḥepê ḥāreb (H6310+H2719, edge/mouth of the sword) — literally 'to the mouth of the sword,' a standard formula for total slaughter (Genesis 34:26; Joshua 6:21; Judges 8:24). The same language is used for Jericho's destruction.
38: wĕhammoʿēd hāyāh lĕʾanšê yiśrāʾēl ʿim hāʾōrēḇ lĕharḇôt maśʾat heʿāšān min-hāʿîr — 'The prearranged signal between the men of Israel and the ambush was that they should send up a great cloud of smoke from the city.' moʿēd (H4150, appointed sign/meeting — the same word as mōʿēd for the Tent of Meeting/appointed feast; here meaning a pre-arranged signal). The smoke signal is the analogue to Joshua 8:19-20, where the smoke over Ai signals the ambushers to close the trap. The use of moʿēd (a sacred-calendar word) for a military signal is not theologically loaded here, but the lexical choice points to the dual register of this word throughout the OT.
39: wĕhāpōk ʾanšê yiśrāʾēl bammilḥāmāh ûḇinyāmîn heḥēl lĕhakôt ḥălālîm bĕʾanšê yiśrāʾēl kĕšĕlōšîm ʾîš — 'When the Israelites turned in battle and Benjamin began to strike down some thirty men of Israel.' A transitional verse describing the feigned retreat phase. The specific number 'about thirty' (kĕšĕlōšîm ʾîš) signals Benjamin's overconfidence — they take thirty when they expected to rout thousands.
40: wĕhammaśśĕʾt heḥēllāh laʿălôt min-hāʿîr tîmĕrat ʿāšān wĕyipen binyāmîn ʾaḥărāyw wĕhinnēh ʾālāh kĕlîl-hāʿîr haššāmāyimāh — 'The pillar of smoke began to rise from the city; the Benjamites looked back — the whole city was ascending in smoke to the heavens.' tîmĕrat ʿāšān (pillar of smoke — H8490+H6227) — the same phrase used in Joel 2:30 for the cosmic signs of the Day of YHWH and in Song of Solomon 3:6 for the bride's procession 'coming up like a column of smoke.' The visual image of Gibeah ascending in smoke is the mirror of Sodom (Genesis 19:28: the smoke of the land went up like the smoke of a furnace) — the Sodom-parallel the narrator established in Judges 19 is now completed visually.
41: wĕʾanšê yiśrāʾēl hāpākû ûḇinyāmîn neḥĕpaz kî rāʾāh kî nāgĕʿāh ʿālāyw hārāʿāh — 'The Israelites turned and the Benjamites were terrified — they saw that disaster had come upon them.' neḥĕpaz (H926, were terrified/dismayed — the Niphal of bāhal, to terrify, be dismayed; cf. Isaiah 21:3, Psalms 6:11) — a strong psychological-fear term. rāʿāh (evil/disaster) — the same word used for Benjamin's evil in Judges 20:12. What they refused to purge from their midst now returns upon them.
42: wayyipnû lipnê ʾanšê yiśrāʾēl ʾel-derek hammidbār ûmilḥāmāh hidbîqattu ûʾăšer min-hĕʿārîm maśḥîtîm ʾôtô bĕtôkô — 'They turned before the men of Israel toward the desert, but the battle caught up with them; and those who came out of the cities destroyed them in the middle.' hidbîqattu (H1692, stuck to/pursued relentlessly — dāḇaq, the same root as Ruth 1:14, Ruth cleaving to Naomi; and Deuteronomy 4:4, clinging to YHWH) — the pursue-and-cling root is here used for a battle catching up with fleeing soldiers, a grim reversal of the covenant-loyalty term.
43: kilĕlû ʾet-binyāmîn rādĕpû minnûḥāh haddĕrîsûhû nōkaḥ giḇĕʿāh mizrāḥ šāmšāh — 'They surrounded Benjamin, pursued from Menuha, trampled them toward the east of Gibeah as the sun rose.' kilĕlû... rādĕpû... haddĕrîsûhû (surrounded... pursued... trampled) — a poetic triplet of destruction verbs. The triplet recalls the Exodus pattern: Egypt surrounded Israel, pursued, then was overwhelmed (Exodus 14:23-28). Here Israel applies the pursuit-and-trampling verbs to their own kindred.
44: wayyippĕlû mibinyāmîn šĕmônāt ʿāśār ʾelep ʾîš kull·ô-ʾēlleh ʾanšê ḥāyil — 'Eighteen thousand from Benjamin fell — all of them men of valor.' ʾanšê ḥāyil (H376+H2428, men of valor/capability) — the honorific used throughout Judges and Ruth for elite fighters. The narrator honors even the fallen enemies with their warrior-dignity designation. The running total: 18,000 here, 5,000 in Judges 20:45, 2,000 more, total 25,100 (Judges 20:35).
45: wayyipnû wayyānusû hammidbārāh ʾel-selaʿ hārimôn wayyĕʿōllĕhû bammĕsillôt ḥămiššet ʾălāpîm ʾîš wayyaddĕbîqû ʾaḥărāyw ʿad giḇĕʿāh ûwayyakû mimmennû ʾalpayim ʾîš — 'They turned and fled toward the rock of Rimmon; 5,000 were cut down on the roads; 2,000 more were pursued to Gibeah.' The rock of Rimmon (selaʿ hārimôn) becomes the refuge for the 600 survivors (Judges 20:47). Rimmon (H7417, pomegranate — the same word as the pomegranate decorations on the temple pillars, 1 Kings 7:18) — a geographical name that carries no special theological resonance here, but the cave-refuge of 600 men will become the seed of Benjamin's survival in Judges 21.
46: wayyĕhî kol-hannoplîm mibinyāmîn ʿĕśrîm wĕḥāmiššāh ʾelep ʾîš šōlēp ḥāreb bayyôm hahûʾ kull·ô-ʾēlleh ʾanšê ḥāyil — 'All who fell from Benjamin on that day totaled 25,000 swordsmen — all men of valor.' The summary verse closes the battle account with the same honorific (ʾanšê ḥāyil) for the fallen. The precision of the total (25,000, matching Judges 20:35's 25,100 count) and the repeated honorific signals that the narrator is being careful to neither dehumanize Benjamin nor glorify their defeat.
47: wayyipnû wayyānusû hammidbārāh ʾel-selaʿ hārimôn šĕš-mēʾôt ʾîš wayyēšĕḇû bĕselaʿ-rimôn ʾarbāʿāh ḥŏdāšîm — 'Six hundred men fled to the rock of Rimmon and stayed there for four months.' šĕš-mēʾôt (600 men) — the number echoes the 600 Danites of 18:11 and, more significantly, the 600 who fled with David at Ziklag (1 Samuel 27:2 — David's core fighting force). The four months of exile at Rimmon creates a narrative pause before Judges 21's resolution attempts. The number 600 will drive the arithmetic of Judges 21: 400 Jabesh-gilead virgins (Judges 20:12) are insufficient, necessitating the Shiloh plan.
48: wĕʾanšê yiśrāʾēl šābû ʾel-bĕnê binyāmîn wayyakkûm leḥepê ḥāreb mēʿîr mĕtōm ʿad-bĕhēmāh ʿad kol-hannimṣāʾ gam kol-hĕʿārîm hannimṣāʾôt śārepû bāʾēš — 'The men of Israel turned against the Benjamites and put them to the sword — men, animals, and all found — and all the cities they found they burned with fire.' The ḥērem-like totality of this destruction — mēʿîr mĕtōm ʿad-bĕhēmāh (from the cities: people to animals) — echoes the ḥērem formulas of Joshua 6:21 (Jericho: people, animals, everything) and 1 Samuel 15:3 (Saul's charge to destroy Amalek totally). There is no divine command for this total destruction; Israel acts on its own authority, and the consequences (Judges 21's wife crisis) follow from the totality of their retribution.
